nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; coral-rich lagoon and seaward reefs at depths of 0–45 m

diet.notes: In the wild, has been documented feeding on filamentous algae, coral polyps, and benthic invertebrates. In aquaria, has been reported to accept finely chopped seafood, mysid shrimp, baby brine shrimp, frozen marine preparations, and algae/spirulina-based foods. Generally advised to feed small amounts at least 3 times daily, particularly during initial acclimation.

imageCandidates.notes: Rickard Zerpe, 2213x1660. Tack-sharp full-body in-situ shot; vivid yellow-tan body with vertical rows of dark spots, dark dorsal bars, orange caudal peduncle and black eye bar all clearly shown.
Bernard E. Picton, 1024x768. Sharp broadside reef shot showing vertical spot rows and dark bars; slightly lower resolution than the pick.
krokodiver, 1785x1339. Sharp, well-lit lateral view; vibrant coloration and diagnostic spot-and-bar pattern clearly visible.

summary: An Indo-Pacific butterflyfish with a yellow-tan body marked by vertical rows of dark spots and seven dorsal bars, generally considered not reef-safe due to documented predation on corals and invertebrates. Best suited to a spacious fish-only system with peaceful tankmates.
careNotes:
The Spotband Butterflyfish (Chaetodon punctatofasciatus) is generally considered an intermediate-level saltwater fish. It is commonly confused with the closely related Sunset Butterflyfish (Chaetodon pelewensis), which shares some of the same trade names — the two are sister species distinguishable mainly by spot pattern: the Spotband carries vertical rows of dark spots, while the Sunset's spots run diagonally. Their ranges also overlap in parts of the Indo-Pacific. The Spotband tends to be shy and docile and has been reported to do well singly or as a bonded pair if both individuals are introduced simultaneously. Like most Chaetodon species, it has been documented to form monogamous pair bonds in the wild, and established pairs are generally advised to be kept together rather than separated. Numerous hiding places among live rock are important for reducing stress, and ample open swimming space is generally advised, as this species has been described as an active swimmer.

This species is commonly considered not reef-safe. It has been documented preying on tubeworms, ornamental clams (including Tridacna clams), and both soft and LPS (large-polyp stony) coral polyps. It should not be housed with corals, anemones, or sessile invertebrates.

Diet is broad — both meaty foods and vegetable matter (marine algae, spirulina) are generally advised, with feedings at least three times daily, especially during acclimation when refusing food is a common early problem.
breedingNotes: Generally regarded as not bred in captivity. In the wild has been reported to form monogamous pairs, with pelagic spawning typical of butterflyfishes; rearing of pelagic larvae has not been documented in the aquarium hobby.
